macro_segments.title: Introduction and Opening of the Mishna on Multiple Violations of Nazir Prohibitions

micro_segments.title: Opening remarks and context of the daf
Mishna: Nazir drinking wine or cutting hair all day without separate warnings
Conceptual issue: are these separate acts or one continuous act?
Distinction: separate warnings create separate violations and separate lashes

macro_segments.title: Three Prohibitions of Nazir Applied to Wine, Hair, and Tumah (Impurity)

micro_segments.title: Parallel cases of drinking wine, cutting hair, and becoming impure all day
Conceptual difference: tumah as status versus process
Critical issue: once already impure, does touching another dead body constitute a separate violation?

macro_segments.title: Rava's Position: Prohibition of Becoming Impure Only Applies When Not Already Impure

micro_segments.title: Rava citing Rav Huna: Torah speaks fully with two separate prohibitions—lo yitama and lo yavo
Analysis: lo yitama focuses on state; lo yavo focuses on the act of entering
Rava's conclusion: tumah and tumah—if already impure, no additional lashes

macro_segments.title: Rav Yosef's Position: Separate Acts of Impurity Incur Separate Lashes Even While Already Impure

micro_segments.title: Rav Yosef's position: even tumah and tumah, separate act with separate warning brings separate lashes
Case of nazir in cemetery touched by multiple corpses while already impure
Rav Yosef's support from the Mishna versus Rava's support from a braita

macro_segments.title: Resolution: Distinction Between Impurity While Actively Touching Versus After Removing Contact

micro_segments.title: Explanation of av hatumah and higher impurity levels during chain-contact
Key principle: tumah bechiburin—higher impurity status while actively touching
Proof from cases of goses and parah adumah: tumah bechiburin is derabbanan, not d'Oraita
Resolution: Rava says during active contact no new violation; removing hand and touching again constitutes new violation

macro_segments.title: Analysis of Lo Yavo (Entering House with Corpse) Relative to Lo Yitama (Becoming Impure)

micro_segments.title: Gemara's question: why do you violate lo yavo when already impure, like touching and touching again?
Rabbi Elazar's answer: hand entering separately versus whole body entering together
Rav Pappa's case: person in a box, friend rips the roof off—tumah and bia come simultaneously
Mar bar Rav Ashi: entering while someone is goses, violations come at same time

macro_segments.title: Question of Whether Impurity Occurs at Goses or Only at Death, and Role of Lehechalo Phrase

micro_segments.title: Tanna: kohen does not violate lehachalo until person dies; Rebbi disputes for nazir
Rav Yochanan versus Reish Lakish on whether goses is included
Gemara's clarification: person does not cause impurity until soul departs, not at goses
Reconciliation: lehachalo applies to status violation at goses, but tumah only at actual death

macro_segments.title: Practical Halacha for Kohanim: Burying Relatives in Cemetery and Secondary Impurity

micro_segments.title: Tosafot's reading: kohen cannot touch other dead bodies same day after removing from relative
Problem: how does kohen exit cemetery without violating through ohel?
Tosafot's solution: bodies buried at cemetery edge to avoid secondary violations

macro_segments.title: Ra'avad's Revolutionary Reading: Rava's Position Is Actually the Lenient One

micro_segments.title: Ra'avad's reinterpretation: strict position (Rav Yosef) permits lashes only during active contact
Ra'avad's reinterpretation: lenient position (Rava) permits no lashes even after removal
Ra'avad's conclusion: we follow Rava; kohanim nowadays who are all impure do not violate upon further contact
Application: Ra'avad's position potentially relevant to modern questions like kohanim becoming doctors

Kohanim desecration (chilul kohen)
